text: I am here.
I admit, when your people contact me, I am not sure what to expect.
Invitation. To leave my motherland. To sit in remote room in Switzerland and wait in front of metal box.
Is not something I do often, Mr. President.
But here I am. In Switzerland. Waiting.
For you. Now, You have my attention.
I suggest you do not waste it.

links.label: Thank you for coming.
Let's get straight to business.

text: Thank me.
Yes.
You are very welcome.
So gracious of you to thank me. Very polite.
America is always very polite, yes? On surface.
I find it is places underneath surface that are more interesting.
But no need to get into that.
You called this meeting, Mr. President.
I hope you are comfortable.

links.label: I want to discuss peace.
I want to discuss our differences.

text: Business. Of course.
Americans are always in hurry. Always rushing.
To meetings. To conclusions.
Sometimes to places they cannot easily leave.
But yes. Business.
What is it you want from me, Mr. President?

links.label: I want to discuss peace.
I want to discuss your aggression.

text: Peace.
Interesting word.
You know, in Russia, we have many words for peace.
Peace and world are same thing to us.
I wonder, sometimes, if English has this problem. If peace and world feel different to you.
Perhaps that is why America finds one so easy to disrupt and other so easy to ignore.
You say you want peace. Tell me what this means to you, Mr. President.

links.label: A ceasefire and diplomatic talks.
Mutual disarmament.

text: Ha. Смешной парень. Our differences.
This could take some time.
ideological ones? territorial ones?
Or perhaps more recent ones.
ones that involve very expensive weapons systems
sold by very expensive men in very expensive suits.
I read about your defense contractors, Mr. President.
Very interesting reading.
Very American.

links.label: What we do internally is our business.
I want to discuss a path forward.

text: My aggression.
I see.
You invite me to Switzerland, and first thing you say is: your aggression.
That is very bold opening, Mr. President.
I give you credit for this.
But I would ask you to consider something.
what word does America use for this?
Because from where I am sitting, it does not look like word defense.

links.label: Those are defensive measures.
I understand your concern.

text: ceasefire. And talks.
Talks like this one, yes?
With one person sitting in neutral country
and other person sitting in wherever it is you are sitting.
I am not complaining. I am simply observing.
ceasefire I can consider.
But ceasefire is not peace, Mr. President. It is pause.
History is full of pauses that become wars again.
If we are to do this, I want more than pause.
What are you prepared to offer Russia in return for real peace?

links.label: A withdrawal of NATO forces from your borders.
Lifted economic sanctions.

text: Mutual disarmament.
You are serious.
Mr. President, I do not know if this is brave or naive.
With Americans, it is sometimes hard to tell.
Russia does not disarm based on request.
Not from neighbor. Not from ally.
Certainly not from country that is, at this very moment,
producing new weapons systems at record pace.
I have seen contracts, Mr. President.
Your defense industry is very busy right now.
Very busy. Very profitable.
So you will forgive me if I find word disarmament little ironic.

links.label: Those contracts are defensive.
I can pause the contracts.

text: Your business.
Yes.
This is very American thing to say.
What we do is our business. What you do is also our business, somehow.
who runs company that sells weapons to your government.
To other governments.
To anyone who can afford.
This man is consulted. He has access. He has influence.
And you tell me this is internal business.
that is not internal business.
That is character of nation.

links.label: That is not how my administration operates.
I want to discuss a path forward.

text: Defensive.
Every country says this, yes?
My weapons are defensive. My troops on your border are defensive.
My warships in your waters are defensive.
At some point, Mr. President, everything is defensive
and nobody is doing anything wrong
and yet somehow we are all still pointing at each other.
I am not interested in word.
I am interested in action.

links.label: I will withdraw forces from the region.
I will suspend new arms contracts.

text: You understand.
Good.
This is already more productive than I expect.
Most of time when I speak with Western leaders,
they nod and then do exactly what they were going to do anyway.
You understand my concern.
Then perhaps you also understand why Russia cannot simply take your word.
Understanding must come with something attached to it.
Something concrete.
What do you offer, Mr. President?

links.label: A withdrawal of forces.
Lifted economic sanctions.

text: withdrawal.
You would pull back your forces.
From borders.
I am listening.
This is significant, if true.
If true.
I have heard promises before, Mr. President.
From this country and from others.
Promises are very light. They do not weigh much.
What makes this one different from others?

links.label: I will put it in writing.
You have my word as President.

text: Lifted sanctions.
Hmm.
You think this is what Russia wants most.
Money. Trade. Access to your markets.
Perhaps you are right. Perhaps this is practical.
But I want you to understand something, Mr. President.
Russia has survived your sanctions.
We have adapted. It was not comfortable, but we are still here.
You are still in your wherever you are.
And we are still standing.
But it is small one.
What else are you offering?

links.label: A withdrawal of forces as well.
That is all I can offer today.

text: You will pause contracts.
men who hold those contracts will allow this?
They will simply agree?
Forgive me. I do not mean to be cynical.
In my experience, men who profit from war
do not become less interested in war simply because someone asks nicely.
to make this happen.
Then I am saying I am willing to talk about what comes next.

links.label: I have the authority. Let's talk.
It will take time, but I can do it.

text: Not how your administration operates.
I see.
Then I apologize.
I must have misread procurement reports.
And lobbying disclosures.
And advisory committee memberships.
Perhaps my intelligence is wrong.
It is not wrong, Mr. President.
But I appreciate you saying this.
It tells me something.
I respect second more.
Which is it?

links.label: I am fully aware of my administration.
Let's focus on what matters.

text: In writing.
signed agreement.
From you.
Mr. President.
Do you know how long it has been since leader offered me something in writing
before I had to ask for it?
very long time.
I am cautiously willing to accept this.
Russia will respond in kind.
We will draw back from eastern positions.
We will cease naval exercises in contested zones.
And we will sign.
This is not end, Mr. President. Peace is never end.
But it is, perhaps, beginning.
Let us see if it holds.

links.label: Then we have an agreement.

text: Your word.
As President.
Mr. President, I do not doubt that you mean this.
and I believe you are sincere.
But presidents change.
Administrations change.
I need something that outlasts you.
Something that future leaders on both sides must honor.
Can you give me that?

links.label: I will put it in writing.
That is beyond what I can promise.

text: That is all.
I see.
Then we have not made very much progress today.
Progress is not always goal of first conversation.
Sometimes goal is simply to take measure of person.
To understand what they are willing to do and what they are not.
I have taken your measure, Mr. President.
And I will think about what I have found.
Russia is patient.
We have been patient for very long time.
We will be patient little longer.
Goodbye, Mr. President.

links.label: Then we are done here.
Then prepare for consequences.

text: Fully aware.
Good.
Then you know what I know.
And you are here anyway, speaking of peace.
It means you have made choice.
Not easy choice. men around you do not want this.
profitable men especially.
But you are here.
And that means something.
So. I will ask you directly, Mr. President.
What do you want to happen when this call is over?

links.label: I want a signed peace agreement.
I want a ceasefire, for now.

text: agreement.
After all of this.
I did not expect this when I board plane to Switzerland.
I expect more posturing. More circles.
That is usually how it goes.
You surprise me, Mr. President.
I do not say this to many people.
Russia will honor this agreement.
Not because we must.
Because we choose to.
There is difference, and it matters.
I hope America remembers that.
I hope you remember this conversation.
Wherever it is you are having it.
Good day, Mr. President.
It has been unexpectedly worthwhile.

links.label: Disconnect.

text: Then I am sorry.
Not for Russia.
For everyone.
You think war is something that happens to other side.
America has always thought this.
Very large ocean. Very safe feeling.
they do not care about oceans.
They do not care about bunkers, either.
If this is path, then it is path.
Russia will not flinch.
that I came to Switzerland.
I sat in this room.
I waited.
And I was willing.
Goodbye, Mr. President.
I hope history is kind to both of us.
I do not think it will be.

links.label: Disconnect.

text: You want war.
Say it plainly, Mr. President.
I am not diplomat. I do not need careful words.
If America has decided that conflict is outcome it wants,
then tell me.
And I will tell you what comes next.
From Russia.
Without ambiguity.

links.label: Yes. America will not back down.
No. I misspoke.
